data.title: Crypto VC business is getting better.
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

Many people believe that crypto VC is heading for its twilight.

Over the past decade, crypto VC has been highly homogenized—crowding into the same sectors, telling the same stories, and vying for the same projects. While seemingly vibrant, the industry is actually fragile.

But what is happening now may be one of the most anticipated moments since the industry's inception: the market is truly differentiating for the first time.

At the end of February 2026, two fundraising announcements appeared one after the other.

On one hand, Dragonfly Capital has completed its fourth fund raising, totaling $650 million, focusing on stablecoins, on-chain financial infrastructure, and the tokenization of real-world assets. On the other hand, Paradigm is seeking up to $1.5 billion in funding for its new fund, expanding its investment scope from crypto to cutting-edge technologies such as AI and robotics. Both are top-tier venture capital firms in the crypto industry, both operating during a downturn, so why have they taken such different paths? The question becomes even more interesting if we also consider a16z Crypto. These three funds represent three drastically different answers offered by crypto VCs in the face of industry challenges. **Guardianship: a16z Crypto's Long-Term Logic** In the crypto VC fundraising landscape, a16z Crypto has long held a top position. This is Andreessen Horowitz's (a16z) dedicated crypto investment fund line, having completed four rounds of fundraising since 2013, totaling over $7.6 billion, making it one of the world's largest crypto funds in terms of fundraising size. Earlier this year, a16z completed a new round of fundraising totaling $15 billion, spanning multiple areas including infrastructure, application layers, and growth funds, and listing the intersection of AI and crypto as one of its key investment directions. Chris Dixon, a partner at a16z Crypto, views blockchain as the next infrastructure of the internet, believing that the crypto industry is in a long "foundational period," much like the neural network paper published in 1943 is to AI today; true mainstream adoption requires decades of groundwork. Dixon has publicly stated that a16z Crypto's current holdings represent 95% of its historical investments, because in venture capital, selling quality assets prematurely is the worst decision. The team's annual crypto industry report consistently sends a signal to investors: even in a market downturn, we remain committed to understanding what's happening in the industry. a16z Crypto targets long-term institutional investors in the crypto fundraising arena—old money with a deep faith in the industry. For them, as long as they still believe in the future of crypto, a16z Crypto is a natural choice.

Transformation: The Financial Evolution of Dragonfly

Dragonfly was founded in 2018, starting as an early-stage crypto VC connecting the Asian and American markets. Its first fund was only $100 million, and its core competitive advantage at the time was the co-founders' ability to leverage geographical arbitrage between the two markets.

Since 2019, Dragonfly has gradually expanded into the secondary market, managing liquidity and building its own trading team. This not only serves as a risk hedging tool but also provides real-time market data for primary market investments, becoming a supplementary perspective for project evaluation.

In 2022, Dragonfly acquired Metastable, a crypto hedge fund co-founded by Naval Ravikant in 2014, bringing it under its wing. This created three parallel business lines: Dragonfly Ventures (primary investment), Dragonfly Liquid (liquidity strategy), and Metastable (hedge fund). The core difference between Dragonfly and pure primary crypto funds lies in the combination of primary VC judgment and secondary market trading capabilities. However, building this system was not an overnight process. Building an investment system spanning both primary and secondary markets means simultaneously constructing two completely different decision-making frameworks, risk control systems, and talent structures—primary markets require in-depth technical judgment of early-stage projects, while secondary markets require precise quantitative capabilities in understanding market microstructures. Dragonfly's previous external recruitment clearly required candidates to possess expertise in delta-neutral hedging and derivatives inventory risk management. Such talent is scarce in the crypto industry, and recruiting from traditional financial institutions requires a lengthy adaptation period. This trading system is a barrier that Dragonfly has built over many years, and it's also the most difficult aspect for other funds to directly replicate. Today, Dragonfly is a transaction-driven institution spanning both primary and secondary markets, managing approximately $4 billion in assets, with a portfolio including unicorns such as Ethena, Polymarket, and Monad Labs. However, behind this success lies a less than optimistic industry trend. According to RootData statistics, the primary crypto market completed $22.73 billion in financing in 2025 (excluding post-IPO and debt financing), a 120.6% increase compared to 2024. However, in terms of the number of financing events, there were only 933 events throughout the year, a 40.3% decrease compared to the previous year, marking a five-year low, and the monthly number of financing events showed an almost unilateral downward trend. The total amount of financing is increasing, but the number of projects receiving financing is decreasing, meaning that money is becoming increasingly concentrated, leaving less and less room for small and early-stage projects.

Dragonfly Managing Partner Haseeb Qureshi believes that past experiments with generalized crypto applications lacking financial attributes have been proven false by the market. The new fund will focus on stablecoins, DeFi, and on-chain financial services.

He stated that the recent growth of investments in Ethena, Polymarket, Rain, and Mesh speaks volumes, "The reach of crypto is about to explode, and we want to support the founders at the heart of it all."

Dragonfly Managing Partner Haseeb Qureshi believes that past experiments with generic crypto applications lacking financial attributes have been proven false by the market. The new fund will focus on stablecoins, DeFi, and on-chain financial services.

He said that the growth of recent investments in Ethena, Polymarket, Rain, and Mesh speaks volumes, "The reach of crypto is about to explode, and we want to support the founders at the heart of it all.

... Dragonfly targets investors who believe in the financialization of blockchain, transaction-driven allocators, and investors with a pragmatic attitude towards crypto. They may not need grand narratives about crypto changing the world; real liquidity and sustainable trading returns are what they need. The key to Dragonfly's path is to follow the trend. The crypto industry is becoming increasingly financialized, and Dragonfly has simply turned this trend into its core competitiveness earlier than others. Breakthrough: Paradigm's Boundary Narrative The story of Paradigm begins with a change in a set of numbers. In 2021, Paradigm raised $2.5 billion, setting a record for the largest single fundraising in the history of crypto funds at the time. In 2024, the third fund shrank to $850 million. This time, the target is $1.5 billion, expanding its investment scope from crypto to AI, robotics, and other cutting-edge technologies. Paradigm's background is rooted in venture capital and incubation. Co-founder Matt Huang, a former Sequoia Capital executive, founded a machine learning startup at age 19 that was acquired by Twitter. Another co-founder, Fred Ehrsam, was a co-founder of Coinbase. The team's strength lies in its early trend judgment and control of technical risks. Patrick Collison, founder of Stripe and a collaborator of Matt Huang, once commented on him: "He is calm, meticulous, and patient—qualities particularly suited to complex technologies with delayed impact." Paradigm's investment portfolio includes early protocols such as Uniswap and Coinbase, and these early bets established its industry position. Paradigm has thus been described by outsiders as "more like a combination of a research lab and an engineering organization than a traditional venture capital firm." After the collapse of FTX, Paradigm spent three years rebuilding. However, the current lack of high-quality early-stage investments in the crypto industry has not been fundamentally improved. For a fund that emphasizes judgment and incubation capabilities, the lack of good projects to invest in is a more fundamental predicament than a decline in market capitalization. Therefore, Paradigm's shift towards AI is by no means a spur-of-the-moment decision. In fact, as early as 2023, Paradigm quietly removed any mention of Web3 from its official website. Matt Huang later explained that "the progress of AI is so interesting that it's impossible to ignore," and stated that encryption and AI are not a zero-sum game; there will be significant overlap between the two. Earlier this year, Paradigm and OpenAI jointly released EVMbench, a benchmark tool for testing whether AI models can identify and patch smart contract vulnerabilities. According to OECD data, global VC investment in AI reached $258.7 billion in 2025, accounting for 61% of total global VC investment, compared to only 30% in 2022. However, looking at a more realistic perspective, Paradigm's shift towards AI has more structural reasons. In the entire crypto VC fundraising landscape, a16z Crypto firmly holds the top tier of long-term funds, while Dragonfly is the most capable trader in the financialization sector. Paradigm's team DNA cannot replicate a16z Crypto's long-term belief narrative, nor is it suited to Dragonfly's trade-driven approach. Its team DNA dictates that it can only tell a story of integration and innovation to attract new funds that have already turned their attention away from pure crypto but are still willing to bet on cross-industry technological integration. This is the underlying motivation for Paradigm's shift, and its only room for differentiation.

Hack VC Managing Partner Alexander Pack (former Managing Partner of Dragonfly) stated that KKR and Bain Capital have both shifted from pure private equity investment to credit and public stocks, and a16z has also established funds targeting various segments of the technology sector. Paradigm's move, like the trend across the industry, signifies that the company is maturing and reintegrating into the broader technology landscape.

Three Paradigms, Three Bets

Putting the three funds together reveals a clear logical divergence.

Each of them answers the same question: During a crypto industry downturn, what allows you, as a fund, to continue existing? a16z Crypto's answer is scale and conviction. Large enough to weather cycles, research deep enough to represent the industry, consistently conveying confidence to the market. Dragonfly's answer is capability and focus. Deeply involved in crypto financialization, using trading capabilities to compensate for the limitations of the primary market, maintaining fund activity during periods of project scarcity. Paradigm's answer is narrative and breaking boundaries. Using a new story of AI and crypto integration to attract investors unreachable by traditional crypto VCs, expanding its boundaries from one industry to a larger wave of technological convergence. Three funds, three different responses. No single paradigm is the final one, nor can any paradigm be easily replicated—ultimately, the stories that can be told are determined by the team's DNA. This may be a sign that crypto VCs are maturing; no longer are thousands crowding onto the same path, but rather each finding its own way. Homogeneous industries are fragile; only when different species emerge can the market truly thrive.

data.title: The underlying structure of Web 4.0: AI is the brain, blockchain is the blood.
data.description: During the Chinese New Year, a discussion about the concept of "Web 4.0" sparked a heated debate on Twitter. This was triggered by a Twitter user named @0xSigil who announced online that he had built a so-called "super-intelligent life form" that could continuously operate, evolve, and self-replicate by earning income without human intervention. Based on this implementation, a new concept has been built online: "Web 4.0". In this new concept, AI is the core driving its continuous evolution and operation. But if it only achieves this, then it's not fundamentally different from the AI ​​we usually see online. The more crucial core here is that it must be completely independent of human intervention. --- After humans give it "life" and provide certain initial conditions, even if it completely abandons human help and feeding, it can still continue to function, survive, and even evolve. To achieve this, it must be able to continuously pay for the tokens required for its operation, pay for the better large models needed for evolution, seek "jobs" on the network and obtain a continuous stream of income, and its income and payment behavior cannot be easily taken away by others (such as humans). All of this can only be achieved through encryption technology. I recall a viewpoint circulating online a while ago: With the rise of AI Agents, the development of Web 2 technology will inevitably keep pace. Therefore, the seemingly unmet needs of AI Agents will soon be satisfied; for example, the needs of AI Agents that are currently difficult to meet will soon be fulfilled. The shortcomings of payment accounts and payment methods: They cannot apply for bank accounts like humans, nor can they engage in general financial activities like humans. However, once AI Agents emerge on a large scale, companies will inevitably target this need and launch payment accounts or payment methods specifically adapted to AI Agents through cooperation with traditional banks or financial institutions. This idea might be achievable, but even if it is, it appears to be a clumsy, half-finished product. The accounts and payment methods implemented in this way remain in human hands, preventing AI Agents from achieving true freedom. This is similar to how artificial satellites can never escape Earth's gravity, only able to orbit the Earth and unable to freely soar and explore space. However, if encryption technology and crypto assets are used, the obstacles and "gravity" that bind AI Agents will naturally be removed. Give them crypto wallets, and their assets cannot be taken away by others; relying on blockchain networks, they can break free from traditional financial institutions and freely achieve seamless global payments on the chain. As for AI Agents seeking "careers" online and aiming for a stable and consistent income, there are even more solutions. In an earlier article last year, I speculated that AI Agents could potentially generate staking income by setting up their own Ethereum nodes, or even more simply, by directly staking their Ethereum in Lido to achieve automatic passive income. So I believe the vision proposed in this "Web 4.0" concept is entirely feasible, and in fact, it can be achieved right now. The more noteworthy point here, in my opinion, is whether this concept can generate a powerful economic scale. This is a key focus I've always paid attention to, and it's a necessary condition for AI + Crypto to truly become a powerful sector, and even more so, a necessary condition for encryption technology to truly revolutionize human society in the future. I have always believed that the number of AI agents will far exceed that of humans in the future, and therefore I believe that the economic entities and scale spawned by AI agents will far surpass those of humans. However, in recent years, although various media outlets have reported on the revenue generated and transactions facilitated by AI agents, these figures seem to be too small to exert a significant influence. This makes me wonder if AI + Crypto is still missing a certain "event" or "singularity"? Only when we encounter that "event" or cross that "singularity" will we witness a miracle? I look forward to the day when this "event" and "singularity" arrive.

data.title: Bitcoin isn't gold yet, but that's precisely your opportunity.
data.description:
Written by: Daii

The war in the Middle East continues, and no one dares to predict when a ceasefire will occur. The market, however, has already provided an answer—not for the war itself, but for "who is the true insurance."

In this round of escalating geopolitical conflict, gold rose due to safe-haven demand, while Bitcoin, amidst risk aversion, first fell below $64,000 before recovering to around $66,400. This is not a dismissive statement like "volatility is normal"; it's more like a cold, hard identification:

In the most tense moment in the world, gold is more like "insurance," while Bitcoin remains more like a "high-volatility, high-risk asset."

If you only understand this as the "bankruptcy of the digital gold narrative," you will miss what this article truly aims to offer—opportunity.

Because the market isn't currently debating whether Bitcoin deserves to be considered gold; it's pricing it using risk budgets: since it's more like a risk asset, it's being discounted. The best illustration of this "discount" isn't forum sentiment, but the footsteps of compliant funds: MarketWatch reports that US spot Bitcoin ETFs have seen net outflows of approximately $2.6 billion since the beginning of 2026, contrasting with net inflows in the same period of 2025. Meanwhile, the World Gold Council (WGC) is equally direct: gold ETFs will continue to attract inflows in 2026, driving global gold ETF total AUM and holdings to record highs. Same world map, same round of macroeconomic anxiety, same batch of global funds: Gold is like a safe haven, Bitcoin is more like "a more volatile exposure." However, please note: this is not the conclusion of this article, but rather its starting point. The real selling point of this article is: Bitcoin is not yet gold—but this is not bad news; rather, it represents a "maturity discount" that can be priced, tracked, and even exploited. In other words: you are not betting on a slogan, you are betting on a process: as Bitcoin gradually crosses the threshold of becoming a "reserve asset," the market will gradually reduce the discount; and discount convergence is often the source of long-term opportunities. The suspense lies here: where does that discount come from? What thresholds must Bitcoin cross before it becomes more like gold than a risk asset when the next war alarm sounds? More importantly: if you are not an institution and do not want to gamble, what rules should you use to position yourself on the side of "discount convergence"? Next, I will break this down with data: first, I will explain why gold can act as insurance during times of war (its size, demand structure, and financial infrastructure), and then I will use the same yardstick to measure Bitcoin—you will see that it does not "lack scarcity," but rather lacks a complete mechanism to turn scarcity into "systemic insurance." And the gap in that mechanism is the source of the discount. 1. Why is gold the best safe-haven asset right now? Many people talk about gold, emphasizing its "ancient," "consensus," and "scarcity." These are not wrong, but they cannot explain the true power of gold in the financial system. Gold's true power comes from three things: Its size is large enough to accommodate global safe-haven funds; its demand structure is stable enough that even a decline in financial demand won't create a vacuum; and its infrastructure is deep enough to facilitate clearing, pledging, financing, and market making within institutional systems. These three things are not adjectives, but numbers. 1.1 First Layer: Gold is "Large Enough" The World Gold Council (WGC) estimates that as of the end of 2025, global above-ground gold reserves will be approximately 219,891 tons. (World Gold Council) This isn't just a year's production; it's the "stock base" accumulated by humanity over a long period. In the words of traders, this means: market capacity. When risks suddenly escalate (such as an escalation of war, a threat to energy supplies, or rising inflation expectations), global funds will undergo "safe-haven rebalancing." If an asset's market capacity is too small, then once funds flood in, they will push the price up; once funds withdraw, they will push the price down. The greater the price volatility, the more it resembles a "spring," and the less it can fulfill the role of "insurance." Gold's stock makes it more like a huge reservoir: the water level rises and falls, but it won't overflow due to a sudden downpour. This is why, during wartime, gold is more likely to be one of the first choices for funds—not because it doesn't fluctuate, but because it doesn't require extreme volatility to secure transactions and exits. (Reuters) 1.2 Second Layer: Gold is "Stable" The same WGC "Ground Stock Structure" data explains the stability of gold very clearly: Jewelry approximately 97,645 tons (44%) Gold bars and coins (including gold ETFs) approximately 50,978 tons (23%) Central banks approximately 38,666 tons (18%) Others approximately 32,602 tons (15%) You can think of this as the "tripod" of gold: The first leg: Jewelry and cultural consumption (slow demand) It doesn't chase highs and lows, reacts slowly, but has a very broad reach. When financial demand recedes, it provides a base that "won't instantly drop to zero." (World Gold Council) The second leg: Investment demand (fast demand) When war, debt, exchange rates, and interest rate expectations change, investment demand quickly pushes gold to the forefront. The WGC's "Gold Demand Trends" states that total gold demand, including OTC, will exceed 5,000 tons for the first time in 2025, emphasizing that investment activity is a key driver of demand growth. (World Gold Council)

The third leg: Official sectors (hard demand)

Central bank holdings make gold's "reserve attribute" not just a slogan, but a long-term allocation on the balance sheet. The WGC's central bank section shows that in 2025, central banks' net gold purchases were approximately 863.3 tons; although lower than the more than 1,000 tons in previous years, it was still described as "resilient." (World Gold Council)

Media outlets such as the Financial Times also discussed this point based on WGC data: central bank purchases have slowed, but investment demand has risen strongly, and overall demand remains high. (Financial Times)

The common effect of this tripod is:

Gold demand is not "only driven by financial speculation." When war causes a sharp drop in risk appetite, the buying of gold doesn't appear out of thin air; it's supported by structural factors, institutional inertia, and long-term holders. 1.3 The Third Layer: Gold is "Deep" The most valuable thing during wartime isn't the return, but "certainty": You need to be certain you can buy, and you also need to be certain you can sell; you need to be certain you can settle, and you also need to be certain the settlement system won't freeze. The trading and clearing data publicly disclosed by the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) provides a concrete picture of the "market depth" of gold: The LBMA's trading report shows that, as of February 20, 2026, the 12-week moving average weekly nominal turnover in the London gold market was approximately $1.02 trillion. The LBMA's clearing data page is even more straightforward: The London market clears an average of over 20 million ounces of gold per day. (LBMA) Translating these two data points into simpler terms: Gold is not a "trading curve," but rather a toolchain "usable within the institutional world": Market makers provide continuous quotes, clearinghouses ensure delivery and book transfers, and custody and account systems handle large transfers. You may not see it every day, but when war suddenly escalates risks, this system becomes a huge advantage: Funds know they can get in and out. (LBMA) 1.4 Summary: Gold's "golden nature" is a systemic capability repeatedly validated by war. Therefore, when war puts the market into risk-off mode, gold is often bought not because it won't fall, but because it possesses three systemic capabilities: Capacity: Large enough to absorb safe-haven funds; Structure: Multi-faceted demand support, including fast investment demand, slow jewelry demand, and central bank demand; Infrastructure: A deep enough trading and clearing network to provide certainty of "entry and exit" during periods of stress. (LBMA)

At this point, you'll find a crucial turning point has naturally emerged:

For Bitcoin to become gold, it's not about shouting the slogan louder, but about gradually developing these systemic capabilities.

Let's use the same yardstick to measure Bitcoin.

2. Why isn't Bitcoin gold yet?

War is actually a kind of "stress test." It doesn't give you time to give long speeches; it just throws assets into an extreme environment to see how they behave: like insurance, or like risk exposure.

(Reuters) Following the escalation of the Middle East conflict in 2026, the starkly different performances of gold and Bitcoin tell us: Gold is gold because it has already achieved "reserve asset status" within the financial system; Bitcoin is still on its way. The most crucial difference on this path, I've broken down into four hurdles. Each hurdle can be explained by data and corresponds to a "maturity discount"—and the opportunity lies in the process of discount convergence. 2.1 Hurdle One: Capacity and Market Depth Let's first look at the "depth" of gold. You don't need to look at the price curve; looking at its "traffic system" is enough. The London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) disclosed that, as of February 20, 2026, the 12-week moving average weekly nominal turnover of the London gold market was approximately $1.02 trillion. (LBMA) What does this mean? It means that gold is an asset that can be "continuously accessed" in the institutional world: market making, clearing, allocation, and hedging all have a sufficiently thick trading and counterparty network to support them. (LBMA) Now let's look at the "depth" of Bitcoin. CoinGecko's real-time page shows that Bitcoin's market capitalization is approximately $1.32 trillion, and its 24-hour trading volume is approximately $42.109 billion. (CoinGecko) You'll notice a very intuitive difference in scale: gold's weekly trading volume in the core institutional trading pools is in the "trillion-dollar range"; Bitcoin's 24-hour trading volume across the entire network is in the "tens of billions of dollars range." (LBMA) Of course, there are differences in terminology (OTC vs. exchange aggregation, weekly vs. daily), but the scale difference itself is enough to illustrate the point: When panic strikes, a true "safe-haven asset" must be able to be bought on a large scale without distortion; it must be able to be sold on a large scale without a stampede. This requires capacity, and even more so, market depth and a clearing network. Therefore, in times of rapidly escalating risk, such as war, gold is more like a "passable highway," while Bitcoin is more like a new road with "many cars, road under construction, but occasional congestion or even sudden bumps." (LBMA) 2.2 Threshold Two: Crisis Window Attributes The most vulnerable aspect of the "like gold" concept is being challenged by one question: What exactly are you providing during a stock market crash? MSCI (Morgan Stanley Capital International) conducted a very direct test in their 2021 report, "Bitcoin: Good as Gold?": When the stock market experienced a significant downturn (they used "monthly return < -3%" as an extreme downturn month), Bitcoin also declined in 8 out of 12 samples, often falling more sharply than the stock market; while gold had positive returns in 8 of these windows. (msci.com) This statement can almost be considered the "functional difference between gold and Bitcoin". In short: low correlation does not equal hedging; the key is whether you are truly hedging when you most need it. In another study published in 2025, MSCI, "Balancing Risk and Return: Gold and Digital Assets in a 60/40 Portfolio," described this in more "portfolio management language": They pointed out that during periods of market stress, gold typically buffers drawdowns; digital assets (represented by their digital asset index) tend to fall more sharply than stocks; and digital assets are more like "recovery assets," contributing more excess returns when volatility subsides and sentiment improves. They even used crisis windows (2008 financial crisis, 2020 pandemic, 2022 Fed pivot, 2025 tariff shock) to illustrate relative performance: gold acts as a buffer, while digital assets act more like a magnifying glass for stress. (msci.com) Combining these two MSCI conclusions and returning to today's war window, you'll better understand the market's "instinctive reaction": When risk rises, gold is bought more like insurance; while Bitcoin is more like a risk exposure, first reduced in position and then supported. (Reuters) This is also an important "opportunity hint": If Bitcoin is to be more like gold, it needs to be "less bad" during periods of stress. When its behavior begins to change during a crisis window, the market's "risk asset discount" for it will loosen. 2.3 Threshold Three: Institutional Demand Anchor Why is gold more stable during panic? Because it has a demand anchor that is difficult to replicate: official sectors. The World Gold Council, in its report "Gold Demand Trends: Full Year 2025," wrote that central banks' net gold purchases in 2025 will reach 863.3 tons; emphasizing that although this is lower than the previous three consecutive years exceeding 1,000 tons, it is still at a historically high level. (World Gold Council) This means that gold's "reserve asset status" is not just private speculation, but a long-standing practice of national balance sheets. (World Gold Council) At the same time, gold also has a very mature and compliant channel for private investment: ETFs. The WGC's US market report details this: US gold demand is projected to grow by 140% year-on-year to 679 tons in 2025, almost entirely driven by gold ETF investments; US gold ETFs attracted 437 tons of demand, pushing total holdings to 2,019 tons, with an AUM of approximately $280 billion. (World Gold Council) Looking at Bitcoin: its most significant change is precisely the emergence of compliant channels (spot ETFs). Bitbo's ETF Tracker shows that as of February 27, 2026, US spot Bitcoin ETFs held a total of 1,272,069 BTC, approximately 6.057% of the 21 million BTC cap, worth approximately $84.75 billion. (bitbo.io) What does this indicate? This indicates that "institutional buying" for Bitcoin is emerging—it's no longer limited to crypto exchanges. (bitbo.io) However, the differences are equally significant: Gold's institutional anchors include central banks and a more mature ETF system; Bitcoin currently relies primarily on ETFs as its institutional entry point, while official institutional anchors are far from forming a sustainable, publicly held structure of equal caliber. (World Gold Council) This is why, during wartime, gold is more like "insurance accepted worldwide," while Bitcoin is more like "a highly volatile asset accepted by some institutions." Because: The anchor isn't heavy enough; the ship is more easily propelled by the waves. (Reuters)

2.4 Threshold Four: Banking System and Prudential Supervision

The final hurdle is the toughest and most easily overlooked by the "digital gold narrative": Can the banking system use Bitcoin like it uses gold?

In its final standard, "Prudential Treatment of Cryptoasset Exposures," the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS) groups banks' exposure to crypto assets and sets strict exposure limits for the higher-risk group (Group 2): the thresholds are based on the bank's Tier 1 capital and are set at thresholds such as 1% and 2%, with the aim of limiting the banking system from introducing highly volatile assets into systemic risk.

(bis.org)

For certain high-risk crypto assets, under the BCBS framework, some Group 2b exposures are subject to a 1250% risk weight / equivalent to a 100% capital reduction effect. (Ashurst)

Translating these regulatory terms into intuition:

Gold is already a universal asset in the banking system that is "collateralizable, financeable, market-making available, and liquidable";
Bitcoin is still in the banking system's area of ​​"high capital costs, limited quotas, and strong prudential constraints."

This directly affects one thing: when panic occurs, who can be used as "collateral" by the banking system to quickly mobilize liquidity?

Gold can; Bitcoin is still difficult to achieve the same level on a large scale.

(bis.org) This is precisely why the phenomenon you see in the war window is not surprising: gold is more like "in-system insurance," while Bitcoin is more like "a highly volatile asset at the edge of the system." (Reuters) 2.5 Summary Now you should understand "why Bitcoin isn't gold yet," because Bitcoin hasn't successfully crossed these four hurdles: Capacity and Depth: Gold has a deeper institutional trading pool. (LBMA) Crisis Window Attributes: Gold is more like a buffer, while digital assets are more like recovery assets. (msci.com)

  • Institutional Demand Anchor: Gold has a solid foundation with central banks and mature ETFs, while Bitcoin's institutional entry point mainly relies on ETFs, and the anchor is still being strengthened. (World Gold Council)

  • Banking System Access: Gold is a universal collateral, while Bitcoin is still under a framework of high capital costs and exposure limits. (bis.org)

    • So, where is your opportunity? It lies in these four discount "convergence paths":

    When the ETF pipeline continues to expand, market depth increases, volatility steps decrease, the availability of regulation and the banking system improves, and (most importantly) its behavior during periods of stress begins to act more like a "shock absorber," Bitcoin will move from "discount pricing of risky assets" to "pricing closer to that of reserve assets."

    When the ETF pipeline continues to expand, market depth increases, volatility steps decrease, the availability of regulation and the banking system improves, and (most importantly) its behavior during periods of stress begins to resemble a "shock absorber," Bitcoin will move from "discount pricing of risky assets" to "pricing closer to that of reserve assets."

    ... (msci.com) 3. Why is "Bitcoin is not gold yet" an opportunity? The window of war is like a spotlight: it doesn't care what you call yourself, it only cares how you are perceived in the panic. After the sudden escalation of the Middle East situation, while gold surged (spot gold once rose to about $5,368/ounce), Bitcoin fell and then stabilized around $66,000. Many people will be frustrated at this point: "See, Bitcoin is not gold at all." However, I want to tell you that frustration alone is not enough; you should see an opportunity: Bitcoin is not gold yet, which means it is still being taxed by the market for maturity. The other side of maturity tax is "maturity discount." The process of discount convergence is itself an opportunity. This statement sounds philosophical, but it's actually the most basic pricing logic in finance: The greater the uncertainty, the larger the discount; as uncertainty decreases, the discount converges. Bitcoin's "uncertainty" isn't mystical; it mainly comes from three quantifiable things: volatility; infrastructure; and institutional acceptance. 3.1 Opportunity 1: Volatility Discounts Are Being Smoothed Out Over Time If gold is insurance, one of its most important qualities is "shock absorption." Bitcoin's most glaring weakness is that it often acts more like an "amplifier." But note: a weakness is not a permanent flaw. A weakness can also be a source of temporary discounts. What you need is not "I believe it will stabilize," but evidence that "it is indeed stabilizing." Two types of evidence are crucial: The first type: evidence of long-term trends. A research article by Fidelity Digital Assets points out that Bitcoin volatility has shown a clear downward trend since its inception, mentioning milestones such as "new lows on an annual scale" and "weekly volatility falling below 75% for the first time in a year," suggesting that volatility will continue to decline as the market matures. (fidelitydigitalassets.com) A similar assessment was made by State Street Global Advisors (SSGA) in February 2026: Bitcoin volatility experienced a significant decline after its early period of extreme high volatility, and has continued its downward trend in recent years. (ssga.com) The second type: evidence of the current state. Glassnode provides the metric of 1-year realized volatility (Realized Volatility 1Y). As of February 22, 2026, this metric was approximately 44.16%. (fidelitydigitalassets.com)

    You should understand that Bitcoin is often categorized as risky precisely because its volatility hasn't yet fallen to a level that "insurance can bear." Once volatility continues to decline, its "configurable share" in institutional risk management models will expand, and the maturity discount will converge.

    This is the first type of opportunity: when volatility decreases, the discount will partially recover.

    3.2 Opportunity Two: Infrastructure Discounts Are Converging

    Gold is like gold, not only because it's large, but also because it can be repeatedly used by institutional systems: market making, clearing, collateralization, financing—the infrastructure is all in place.

    For Bitcoin to become "gold," it also needs one thing: to make the risk manageable. And the most direct tools for managing risk are derivatives and hedging infrastructure.

    CME Group's Bitcoin product page emphasizes that its futures are based on a standardized, regulated reference rate (CME CF Bitcoin Reference Rate) and offer futures and options instruments to help market participants manage Bitcoin price risk. (cmegroup.com) What does this structurally mean? It means the Bitcoin market is beginning to follow a very "traditional" and mature path: More participants → Greater hedging demand → More abundant instruments → More controllable risk → Potential for reduced volatility → More suitable for allocating funds. The early Bitcoin market was like an uninsured highway exit: cars were going fast, but braking could easily lead to a chain reaction of collisions. The emergence of hedging instruments and regulated reference rates is like adding guardrails, emergency lanes, and patrol cars to the highway. It won't prevent you from ever having an accident, but it will reduce the probability of systemic loss of control—and this is one of the reasons why the "maturity discount" converges. This is the second type of opportunity: the more complete the infrastructure, the more controllable the risk, and the easier it is for the discount to converge. 3.3 Opportunity Three: The Existence of Compliant Channel ETFs When you ask "Why isn't Bitcoin gold yet?", the easiest thing to overlook is not volatility, but the "channel." Gold can quickly absorb safe-haven funds during crises because the channel for funds to enter gold already exists and is wide enough. One of the most important changes for Bitcoin is that it finally has a "channel for traditional funds"—spot ETFs. A CoinDesk report on February 27, 2026, noted that US spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded approximately $1.1 billion in net inflows over three consecutive days, with BlackRock's IBIT contributing a significant portion. (coindesk.com) However, it's also important to acknowledge the other side of the coin: these inflows are not stable; they fluctuate with risk appetite and the macroeconomic environment—which is why Bitcoin may still be sold off first during wartime, while gold is more of a safe-haven asset. (Reuters) So, what exactly have ETFs changed? They haven't changed "short-term price fluctuations," but something deeper: the framework for discussing pricing mechanisms. When the pipeline emerged, two groups of people were forced into the same dialogue: One group consisted of "long-term asset allocators," who cared about volatility, correlation, and crisis window performance; the other group consisted of "Bitcoin native believers/holders," who cared about scarcity and long-term monetization. ETFs connected these two groups. Thus, Bitcoin's future will increasingly resemble the "growth history of mature assets," rather than just the "growth history of an insider narrative." This is the third type of opportunity: the existence of the pipeline means a structural pathway for the "convergence of maturity discounts." 3.4 Summary The war window tells you: Bitcoin is not yet gold. However, you should also draw a more useful, clearer, and more restrained conclusion: Bitcoin's opportunity doesn't come from "it's already gold," but rather from "it's not gold yet." Because "not gold yet" means the market is still discounted: volatility discount, infrastructure discount, institutional discount. When these discounts converge, even without discussing any "endgame narrative," its pricing will undergo a structural change. This is because Bitcoin, with its continued downward volatility trend, improved hedging tools, and expanded ETF pipelines, is moving towards a more mature asset class. 4. How to seize the "opportunity" to capture the "maturity discount"? War cheapens all narratives because it exposes risks to you, forcing you to choose: either you pre-define the rules, or you improvise amidst volatility. The standard market reaction after the escalation of the Middle East conflict tells you: don't treat Bitcoin as insurance. It hasn't yet developed into a "stress asset" like gold. But opportunities arise precisely from this: you can treat it as an "option," participating in its maturation process in a controlled manner. Below, I won't offer slogans, only "executable rules." 4.1 Positioning Based on "Budget" Rather Than "Faith" The biggest real hurdle for Bitcoin is volatility. The latest value of Glassnode's 1-year realized volatility (rolling 365 days, annualized) on March 1, 2026, was approximately 43.91%. (studio.glassnode.com) This isn't an "opinion," it's the actual path the market has taken over the past year: holding it is equivalent to buying a "high volatility ticket." How should you use this ticket? The most practical method is to give yourself an intuitive model of "risk budget": You can think of your position size as "weight" and volatility as "road conditions." The bumpier the road (the greater the volatility), the easier it is for a vehicle of the same weight (the same position size) to go out of control. So, on bumpy roads, the car needs to be lighter to avoid being thrown off the curve. Many people lose here: they didn't misjudge the direction, but rather their positions were too large, causing them to be kicked out of the game by volatility. J.P. Morgan Private Bank's statistics on January 30, 2026, provide a very clear "risk management" reminder: Over the past decade, Bitcoin's annualized volatility has been close to 70%, roughly four times that of global equities (approximately 16%); during the same period, Bitcoin experienced 14 "bear market pullbacks" (declines of ≥20%), while global equities only experienced 2; Bitcoin's five most severe declines averaged a 57% drawdown, while global equities' five most severe declines averaged 21%. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) This isn't an emotion; it's the "road condition" you have to accept when you buy your ticket to enter the market. More importantly, there's the difference in behavior during stressful periods: In the same J.P. Morgan statistics, under "risk-off" conditions, Bitcoin falls 93% of the time, while gold falls 55%; during these risk-off periods, equities fall by an average of 8%, Bitcoin falls by an average of 13%, while gold actually gains by an average of +0.4%. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) This explains why gold acts like insurance and Bitcoin like a risk asset during wartime—because historically, it's been treated this way more often. Therefore, position sizing shouldn't be about "how much I believe," but rather "how much risk I can afford to contribute." J.P. Morgan also offers a very useful quantitative intuition: when adding Bitcoin or gold to a traditional 60/40 portfolio, with the same 5% weighting, gold contributes approximately 2% of the portfolio risk, while Bitcoin contributes approximately 13%; a 10% Bitcoin weighting can even contribute 32% of the portfolio risk. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) In simpler terms, Bitcoin's risk contribution is often 2–3 times or even higher than its weighting. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) Therefore, your first rule should be: first, assign a maximum acceptable risk to the portfolio (your own "risk budget"), then work backward to determine the Bitcoin position. It's not about "allocating as much as you want to earn," but rather "allocating as much as you can withstand." 4.2 Putting "Rebalancing" into the Rules For assets like Bitcoin, the biggest enemy is often not its price drop, but the wrong actions you take when it drops: either panic selling or over-spending. MSCI research provides very clear evidence of "portfolio engineering": Over the past 20 years, moving 5% of the weight in a 60/40 portfolio from stocks to gold reduced portfolio volatility from 10.7% to 9.9%, and the maximum drawdown from 33% to 30%, improving the risk-reward ratio. (msci.com)

    In the historical sample of the digital asset index (emphasizing monthly rebalancing), if 5% of the weight is moved from stocks to digital assets, the annualized return increases from 9.2% to 11.9%, while the risk only slightly decreases from 12.1% to 12.2%; with a 10% weight, the return increases to 14.4%, and the risk increases to 13.2%. (msci.com)

    MSCI specifically points out that monthly rebalancing and low short-term correlation can help "tame" the high volatility of digital assets. (msci.com)

    You don't have to copy these proportions exactly (that would be "advice" rather than "science"), but you should incorporate the methodology into your personal rules:

    When you are emotionally stable, pre-agree on the rebalancing frequency (e.g., monthly/quarterly) and commit to adhering to the rules.

    Thus, the greater the volatility, the more you are "selling high and buying low," rather than being driven by volatility. This rule is particularly evident during wartime: when panic sets in, subjective judgments are most easily distorted; pre-set rules are more reliable. 4.3 Clearly Define "Pipeline Risk" If you want to participate in "maturity discount convergence," you must acknowledge that the pipeline itself is also part of the risk. Gold is more like insurance during wartime, partly because its "pipeline" is extremely mature: ETF inflows, OTC depth, and clearing networks are all robust. (World Gold Council) The compliance channels for Bitcoin are thickening, and the following data is worth reiterating: As of February 27, 2026, US spot Bitcoin ETFs held a total of 1,272,069 BTC, approximately 6.057% of the 21 million BTC cap, worth approximately $84.75 billion. (bitbo.io) This illustrates the fact that more and more traditional funds are holding Bitcoin in an "institutionalized way." However, don't forget what this article has consistently emphasized: during periods of stress, funds may still withdraw first, which is one of the reasons why it's not yet like gold. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) Therefore, your third rule should be: Clearly define your "pipeline" for participating in Bitcoin and pre-define: when the market enters a risk-off phase, do you need liquidity, and can you withstand repeated fluctuations in the flow of funds within the pipeline? This isn't a technical detail; it's a survival detail. 4.4 Treating "Institutional Boundaries" as Long-Term Variables Gold's "insurance properties" have an underlying layer that's often overlooked by ordinary people: it has long been used as a usable collateral asset in the financial system. For Bitcoin to approach this layer, it must cross the hard boundary of prudent regulation. BCBS's final standard explicitly states that banks' total exposure to Group 2 crypto assets needs to be kept below 1% of Tier 1 capital; if exceeded, the excess will be subject to stricter capital treatment; and a second threshold of 2% is introduced, beyond which all Group 2 exposure will be subject to stricter treatment. (bis.org) The implication is straightforward: the system's main engine (bank balance sheets) remains very cautious about unpegged crypto assets. Therefore, your fourth rule is: Treat "institutional acceptance" as a long-term variable, not a short-term gamble. It will change the long-term pricing framework of Bitcoin, not just today's or tomorrow's price fluctuations. 4.5 Summary War is not something everyone can predict, but everyone can do something more realistic: write down their rules before the storm arrives. If you treat Bitcoin like gold, you will repeatedly experience the same pain: in the risk window, it acts more like a risky asset, and you will doubt yourself, the narrative, and the world. (privatebank.jpmorgan.com) If you treat it as an "option," and constrain yourself with risk budgets, rebalancing rules, pipeline choices, and institutional boundaries, you are no longer participating in emotional fluctuations, but in the long-term convergence of "maturity discounts." Conclusion War will not end the way we hope, and the market will not proceed according to anyone's narrative. When the alarm sounds, funds first seek "insurance," then talk about "ideals." So what this article wants to leave you with isn't the comforting phrase "Bitcoin will eventually become gold," but rather: "Bitcoin isn't gold yet" isn't bad news, it's a discount. The later the consensus, the greater the opportunity. But a discount isn't a gift; it's a test. Those who can turn discounts into profits never rely on passion, but on understanding the rules. Now, you can do three things—not tomorrow, not next time, but today: First, clearly define your role. Don't let optionality masquerade as insurance: gold provides shock absorption, Bitcoin provides upward elasticity. Once you've misplaced your position, the market will correct you when the next risk window arrives, and often in a ruthless way. Second, write down the rules. What is your maximum acceptable drawdown? Is your rebalancing frequency monthly or quarterly? Are you using an ETF or self-managed funds? Write these three points on a piece of paper and post it where you can see it. The more volatile the market, the less you should trust improvisation. Third, keep an eye on the indicators. When risks rise again, will Bitcoin be the first to be liquidated? Has its volatility decreased? Are ETF fund flows more stable during periods of stress? You should be focusing on whether the discount is converging, not what slogans others are shouting today. Finally, here's a piece of advice that should be your guiding principle: The future doesn't belong to those who "predict correctly," but to those who "live long enough." Position yourself correctly, write down the rules strictly, and stay at the table—only then will you deserve the reward when the market takes back the discount.

data.title: Trump may bypass Congress and forcefully push for a new order for stablecoins in the United States.
data.description:
While the Middle East conflict continues and geopolitical competition intensifies, the financial front within the United States has also entered a standoff.

US President Trump publicly named traditional banks, accusing them of attempting to block the GENIUS Act and delay the CLARITY Act.

On March 4th, Trump posted on Truth Social, stating that if legislation stalls, the US may lose its status as the "crypto capital," and emerging industries will flow to countries like China. He even directly addressed banks, demanding they reach an agreement with the crypto industry, emphasizing that this is in the best interests of all Americans.

This is no longer a legislative disagreement.

This is a direct confrontation between the White House and the banking system.

The core of the conflict lies in a very specific issue: if stablecoins pay returns on balances, are they considered banks? The traditional banking camp, represented by JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon, has a very clear stance. They believe that as long as they hold customer balances and pay returns, they are essentially accepting deposits and paying interest. Accepting deposits is precisely the privilege of banks. Allowing non-bank institutions to conduct "deposit-like business" without FDIC deposit insurance, capital adequacy requirements, or liquidity regulations constitutes unfair competition. The banks' core demand is actually quite direct: either don't pay interest, or be regulated like banks. This is a typical "institutional defense." The White House's Counterattack Faced with pressure from banks, White House digital asset advisor Patrick Witt immediately responded publicly. He stated that what truly needs bank-style regulation is not the "payment of returns" itself, but rather—whether the underlying US dollars are lent out or re-collateralized. According to the GENIUS Act, stablecoin issuers are explicitly prohibited from: Lending; Re-collateralizing; Using reserve funds for venture capital investments. This means that stablecoin reserves are "full reserves," not "partial reserves." In other words, banks create money through credit expansion, while stablecoins are merely digital representations of the US dollar. If they don't create credit, they cannot be defined as banks. What the two sides are actually fighting over is the right to interpret the concept of "bank." The CLARITY Act is deadlocked in Congress. But real progress is unfolding within the executive branch. Paul Atkins has been appointed Chairman of the SEC, and Michael Selig is in charge of the CFTC; the two regulatory agencies are coordinating their regulatory framework. The market generally expects: A draft will be submitted this fall, and the final version will be completed in the spring of 2026. Even though Congress is still in a state of flux, Trump's appointment and the executive branch's push still have a chance to push for the implementation of regulatory rules, thereby taking the lead in restructuring the market. The core of a bank's profit is low-cost liabilities. Once stablecoins are allowed to pay returns on balances, even just a portion of them, they will become a new type of liquidity pool that does not bear credit risk, is not subject to capital constraints, but can still absorb funds. This isn't a matter of innovation. This is competition on the liability side. If the stablecoin system gains institutional approval, banks will face for the first time a competitor that "legally exists but doesn't create credit." This will weaken the stickiness of traditional deposits and change the cost of funds structure. The Trump administration and the crypto industry share the same logic: stablecoins can be a "payment layer tool," not a "credit creation machine." However, the implementation of this consensus also puts unprecedented institutional pressure on the banking system, and traditional rules may be rewritten at any time. Trump's public criticism of banks obstructing the bill actually reveals a larger signal. If stablecoins are viewed as: a source of demand for US Treasury bonds, a global payment tool, and a channel for the expansion of the digital dollar, then it is not merely a matter of financial innovation, but a strategic financial instrument. Once it enters the realm of national competition, the industry interests of banks may not be the highest priority. The beginning of the offensive and defensive battle: Whoever defines "bank" defines the flow of funds. These issues determine not only the future of stablecoins, but also the power boundaries of the US financial system in the digital age. If administrative rules are finalized first, the United States will establish a systematic regulatory framework for decentralized finance networks for the first time. This will bring about a profound change: the digital expansion path of the US dollar will no longer rely entirely on the traditional banking system. This is not a simple regulatory optimization, but a rebalancing of the financial power structure. The conflict has become public, and the rules have not yet been implemented. But what is certain is that this standoff will not end easily.
